hook: A 1901 National Romantic office block crawling with stone trolls, bears and gnomes from the Kalevala.
facts: Built 1899-1901 as the headquarters of the Pohjola Insurance Company at Aleksanterinkatu 44 / Mikonkatu 3.
Primarily designed by the firm Gesellius, Lindgren & Saarinen; it was their first commercial building.
Faced in rough-hewn soapstone, red granite and serpentine, carved with vegetation, squirrels and figures from the Kalevala, Finland's national epic.
The main entrance was sculpted by Hilda Flodin, a pupil of Rodin, and is flanked by carved devils, monsters and trolls.
Bears, the insurance company's symbol, top the pilasters and recur in the interior decoration.
'Pohjola' is the mythic far-northern land of the Kalevala, ruled by a witch (Louhi).
legend: Folklore says passers-by like to wedge cigarette butts into the open mouths of the grotesque Pohjola figures on the facade.
w: A 1901 National Romantic office block crawling with stone trolls, bears and gnomes lifted from the Kalevala. Gesellius, Lindgren and Saarinen faced it in rough soapstone and granite; Hilda Flodin, a pupil of Rodin, carved the devils around the door. Bears -- the insurer's emblem -- top the pilasters.

hook: A Lutheran church dynamited straight into a granite outcrop, roofed by a hovering copper dome.
facts: Lutheran church at Lutherinkatu 3 in the Toolo district, designed by brothers Timo and Tuomo Suomalainen.
Excavation began in February 1968 and the church was consecrated in September 1969.
Carving it out removed about 12,400 cubic metres of bedrock using roughly 4,100 kg of dynamite.
The interior dome is lined with about 22 kilometres of copper wire and ringed by 180 skylights that admit natural light.
Its rough, near-unworked rock walls give it celebrated acoustics, and it is heavily used as a concert venue.
It draws around half a million visitors a year, making it one of Helsinki's most-visited sights.
w: A Lutheran church dynamited straight into a granite outcrop, roofed by a copper dome that seems to hover. The Suomalainen brothers blasted out 12,400 cubic metres of bedrock in 1968-69; the raw rock walls give it famous acoustics, and it doubles as a concert hall. Copper wire lines the dome, ringed by 180 skylights.

hook: A windowless wooden ovoid on a busy square, built purely as a refuge of silence.
facts: Stands on Narinkkatori square at Simonkatu 7, beside the Kamppi shopping centre.
Designed by Mikko Summanen, Niko Sirola and Kimmo Lintula of K2S Architects.
Opened in June 2012, when Helsinki was World Design Capital.
Curved spruce-clad shell about 11.5 metres tall, enclosing roughly 352 square metres.
Conceived as a non-denominational space for quiet and reflection rather than services.
The smooth interior is lined in oiled alder; no events or talking are intended inside.
w: A windowless wooden ovoid set on a busy square, built for one purpose: silence. K2S Architects clad its curved spruce shell, 11.5 metres tall, for Helsinki's 2012 design-capital year. Inside, oiled alder and no talking -- a non-denominational refuge from the city, with no service and no noise. An egg of quiet, beside a shopping mall.

hook: An 8.5-metre pink boy who urinates a heated jet of water all year, even through the Finnish winter.
facts: Sculpture by Finnish artist Tommi Toija depicting a giant naked, urinating boy.
Stands 8.5 metres (28 ft) tall and weighs about seven and a half tons.
First shown at the OpenArt festival in Orebro, Sweden in 2013.
Installed at Helsinki's Market Square in August 2014, where it urinated toward the sea by the Presidential Palace.
Moved to Jatkasaari (Tyynenmerenaukio square) in 2015, near the West Harbour terminal.
Its water flow is heated so the figure can keep urinating year-round.
w: An 8.5-metre pink boy who urinates a heated jet of water all year -- through the Finnish winter and all. Tommi Toija's giant, faintly sheepish nude first peed toward the sea by the Presidential Palace in 2014, then moved out to Jatkasaari by the West Harbour. Seven and a half tons of deadpan. The flow is heated so he never has to stop.

hook: A photo-bait warren of optical illusions, tilted rooms and gravity-defying tricks.
facts: Interactive illusion museum at Fabianinkatu 29, between Senate Square and the Esplanade.
Opened in March 2025; by mid-2026 it had passed roughly 200,000 visitors in its first year.
Holds more than 40 hands-on exhibits exploring perception and paradoxes.
Signature installations include a Zero Gravity Room and a Paradox Tunnel.
Part of the international Paradox Museum chain of illusion venues.
w: A photo-bait warren of optical illusions -- tilted rooms, a gravity-defying tunnel, a zero-gravity chamber where up stops meaning much. Over 40 hands-on tricks of perception fill it, between Senate Square and the Esplanade. It opened in 2025 and pulled 200,000 visitors in its first year. A good place to lose your bearings on purpose.

hook: The university's own museum of curiosities, formed by merging a clutch of older department museums.
facts: Exhibition centre occupies the corner of the University of Helsinki Main Building, entrance at Fabianinkatu 33.
The museum was created in 2003 by merging several separate university collection museums.
It moved out of the Arppeanum building on Snellmaninkatu in 2014 and reopened at the Main Building in 2015.
Now branded 'Flame', it spans three venues: the exhibition centre, the Observatory and the Art Room.
Collections once held at Arppeanum seeded the National Museum of Finland and the Finnish National Gallery.
Open year-round to visitors.
w: The university's own museum of curiosities, stitched together in 2003 from a clutch of old department collections. It left the Arppeanum in 2014 for a corner of the grand Main Building. The stuff once kept here seeded the National Museum and the National Gallery; what remains -- branded 'Flame' -- spans a centre, observatory and art room.

hook: A free Victorian-era glasshouse of palms and cacti just north of Töölönlahti Bay.
facts: Public greenhouse at Hammarskjoldintie 1 A, near the northern end of Toolonlahti Bay by the Olympic Stadium.
Founded in 1893 and free to enter ever since.
Holds more than 200 plant species across three rooms: a Cactus Room, a Palm Room and the Western Wing.
Its oldest specimen is a camellia brought to the garden in 1907.
An outdoor rose garden alongside it blooms from July to September.
Owned and run by the City of Helsinki.
w: A free Victorian glasshouse of palms and cacti just north of Toolonlahti Bay, open to all since 1893. Three rooms -- cactus, palm and the Western Wing -- shelter over 200 species; the eldest, a camellia, arrived in 1907. An outdoor rose garden blooms beside it through high summer. A warm, fragrant escape from the Nordic dark.

hook: An 18th-century manor on a hill above the Vantaa river, said to be walked by a White Lady.
facts: The manor house dates from the 1790s and stands on a hill surrounded by fields beside the Vantaanjoki river in northern Helsinki.
Ownership passed in 1845 to the Russian merchant family Kavaleff.
Jakob Kavaleff sold the manor to the City of Helsinki in 1917.
The manor was renovated and converted into a museum in 1960-61, regaining its original late-1700s appearance and colour.
Today the grounds host a riding school and the manor is run as a cafe/restaurant and event venue.
legend: Oral tradition holds that the manor is haunted by Natalia Kavaleff (later Stjernschantz, 1831-1858), remembered as the 'White Lady' ghost.
w: An 18th-century manor on a hill above the Vantaa river, ringed by fields and, the story goes, walked by a White Lady. Built in the 1790s, it passed to the Russian Kavaleffs in 1845, to the city in 1917. Restored to its Gustavian colours, it runs now as a cafe and a riding school. The ghost is said to be young Natalia Kavaleff, dead at 27.

hook: A Kruununhaka building at the corner of Vironkatu and Meritullinkatu, famous for its headless-colonel ghost.
facts: The building stands at the corner of Vironkatu and Meritullinkatu in the Kruununhaka district, addressed as Vironkatu 1.
The property today belongs to the University of Helsinki and houses university researchers' offices.
Kruununhaka is one of Helsinki's oldest districts and the building features on the Helsinki City Museum's Kruununhaka heritage trail.
legend: Local legend names the resident ghost 'Aleksi', a Russian marine officer said to have lived in the building in the early 1900s and to have hanged himself after finding his wife with another man.

He is said to be heard talking in empty rooms, to ride the lift holding his head under his arm, and to leaf through researchers' papers; reports also describe heavy footsteps and an oppressive atmosphere.
w: A corner building in old Kruununhaka, on the city museum's heritage trail -- and famous for its ghost. Legend names him Aleksi, a Russian marine officer said to have hanged himself here after finding his wife with another man. They say he rides the lift with his head under his arm, leafs through papers, paces empty rooms.

hook: Helsinki's 1879 imperial opera house, built from the rubble of a bombed Baltic fortress.
facts: Construction began in 1876 and the theatre was completed in 1879, opening on 30 March 1880 with Gounod's Faust.
It was built to serve Helsinki's Russian garrison and is at Bulevardi 23-27 in the Kamppi area.
Building stone/brick was brought from the Bomarsund fortress in Aland, destroyed in the Crimean War.
The Finnish National Opera occupied the house until 1993, when it moved to the new Opera House on Toolo bay.
The Alexander Theatre still operates today as a performing-arts venue.
legend: The theatre is reputedly haunted by the ghost of a lonely Russian officer, a war casualty.
w: Helsinki's imperial opera house of 1879, built for the city's Russian garrison -- partly from the rubble of the Bomarsund fortress, blown apart in the Crimean War and shipped from Aland. It opened with Gounod's Faust and held the National Opera until 1993. A lonely Russian officer's ghost is said to haunt it.

hook: Finland's National-Romantic national theatre, reputedly home to half a dozen named ghosts.
facts: The granite National-Romantic theatre building was completed in 1902, designed by architect Onni Tarjanne.
It stands on the north side of Helsinki's Railway Square at Lantinen Teatterikuja 1.
Inside, a monument to the actress Ida Aalberg (1857-1915) is a notable feature.
It is the oldest Finnish-language professional theatre in the country and remains in active use.
legend: The theatre is said to host several ghosts; a 'Grey Lady' is sometimes identified as Ida Aalberg, though other accounts leave her identity unknown.
Other reputed apparitions include Bertta Lindberg on the main stage and women's dressing room, Kaarlo Bergholm backstage, and Jalmar Rinne on the small stage.
w: Finland's National-Romantic national theatre, a granite pile of 1902 by Onni Tarjanne on the railway square -- the country's oldest Finnish-language stage. A monument to the great actress Ida Aalberg stands inside. And it keeps a fuller cast than the living: a Grey Lady, sometimes called Aalberg.

hook: A grim 19th-century brick-gothic county prison on Katajanokka, now a hotel keeping its cells in the basement.
facts: The current prison building dates from 1888, in a deliberately sinister late-19th-century brick gothic style; its on-site chapel is among Helsinki's oldest churches.
Former president Risto Ryti and several government ministers were detained here after being convicted in the war-responsibility trials following the Continuation War.
The prison was hit by an air strike on 6 February 1944, killing one guard and allowing five prisoners to escape during the resulting fire.
The prison closed in 2002 and, after restoration, reopened as a hotel in May 2007, retaining preserved cells and the original chapel.
legend: As a former prison, the hotel attracts ghost rumours tied to its history of incarceration.
w: A grim brick-gothic county prison of 1888 on Katajanokka, now a hotel, its cells and old chapel preserved. President Risto Ryti was held here after the war-responsibility trials; a 1944 air strike killed a guard and let five prisoners flee in the fire. It closed in 2002 and reopened as a hotel in 2007 -- ghost rumours included.

hook: The preserved Kaivopuisto villa where Marshal Mannerheim lived for nearly three decades.
facts: The villa, originally built in 1874, was the home of Marshal of Finland Baron C. G. E. Mannerheim (1867-1951) from 1924 until his death in 1951.
It stands at Kalliolinnantie 14 in the Kaivopuisto district of Helsinki.
Apart from a few rooms adapted for exhibition, the home is preserved in its original state with his furnishings and collections.
It operates as a museum with guided tours, typically open Friday to Sunday.
w: The Kaivopuisto villa where Marshal Mannerheim -- soldier, regent, wartime commander, president -- lived from 1924 until his death in 1951, kept much as he left it. His furniture, weapons and travel trophies fill rooms barely touched since. Open a few days a week, by guided tour. An intimate look at the man behind Finland's national myth.

hook: Finland's sole surviving WWII submarine, beached as a museum on Suomenlinna's Artillery Bay.
facts: Vesikko was launched on 10 May 1933 at the Crichton-Vulcan dock in Turku.
Ordered via a Dutch firm acting as a German front company, she served as the prototype for the German Type II U-boats.
She took part in the Winter War and Continuation War (1939-1944), her only confirmed kill being the Soviet merchant ship Vyborg.
Vesikko was the only Finnish submarine not scrapped after the war; she was moved to Susisaari island in Suomenlinna and restored.
She opened as a museum on 9 July 1973 and is run by the Military Museum of Finland, open in the summer months.
w: Finland's only surviving WWII submarine, beached as a museum on Suomenlinna. Launched in 1933, she was secretly a German prototype -- ordered through a Dutch front company and the model for the Type II U-boats. She fought the Winter and Continuation Wars, sinking a Soviet freighter. Spared the scrappers, she opened to visitors in 1973.

hook: Helsinki's oldest documented execution site, on the Arabia shoreline by the Bokvillan villa park.
facts: The park sits on the Arabianranta shoreline beside Bokvillan, a wooden villa built in 1872 at Hämeentie 125 that now serves as a community and event house.
An execution ground known as Gallows Rock (Hirsipuunkallio) is recorded along the Arabianranta shore from the 16th century; sources give a starting date of 1550.
Early Helsinki had no executioner of its own, so professional executioners travelled from Turku and Hämeenlinna to carry out sentences.
Bokvillaninpuisto is today a public leisure park in the Arabianranta district.
legend: Tradition holds that thieves were hanged and murderers (reportedly often child-killers) beheaded here, and that nobles tied to King Sigismund III met their end on the rock.
w: A quiet shoreline park on the Arabia coast -- and Helsinki's oldest recorded execution ground, the Gallows Rock noted here from 1550. Early Helsinki kept no executioner of its own, so the hangmen came up from Turku and Hameenlinna to do the work. Tradition has thieves hanged and murderers beheaded on the rock. Now just lawn and water.

hook: A rocky hill crowned by Engel's 1834 observatory, with harbour views and a grim folk reputation as a former gallows slope.
facts: Tähtitorninvuori is a rocky hill about 30 metres high in Ullanlinna, next to the South Harbour.
The University of Helsinki Observatory atop the hill was designed by C. L. Engel with professor F. W. A. Argelander and completed in 1834.
In 1748-1750 a small fortress, Ulrikasborg, was built on the hill and named after Queen Ulrika Eleonora; it was dismantled during the Finnish War (1808-1809).
Robert Stigell's bronze sculpture 'Haaksirikkoiset' (The Shipwrecked, 1897) stands on the park's southern edge overlooking the harbour.
The hill is now a public park used for May Day celebrations and winter sledding.
legend: Local tradition describes a pillory and execution site on the hill's slopes in the 1600s, though the documented history centres on its later use as a fortress and observatory.
w: A rocky hill over the South Harbour, crowned by Engel's 1834 observatory and falling away to sea views. A little fortress, Ulrikasborg, stood here in the 1740s before it was pulled down; Stigell's bronze 'The Shipwrecked' watches the water from the southern edge. Folk memory adds a darker note -- a pillory and gallows slope in the 1600s.

hook: A forested island museum of historic wooden buildings dismantled across Finland and rebuilt beam by beam.
facts: The open-air museum was founded in 1909 on the north-east part of Seurasaari island, west of Meilahti.
The first relocated structure was Karuna Church, moved to the island in 1909 to mark the museum's beginning.
The site assembles roughly 87 buildings, ranging from crofts and farmsteads to manor houses, original structures dismantled and reassembled rather than replicas.
The buildings illustrate Finnish rural life from the 18th to the 20th centuries.
A public folk park had already been established on the island in 1889, two decades before the museum.
w: A forested island museum of old wooden Finland, founded in 1909 -- crofts, farmsteads, a manor and a church, around 87 buildings dismantled across Finland and rebuilt here beam by beam. Karuna Church came first. They are originals, not replicas, showing rural life from the 1700s on. A whole vanished countryside, gathered on one island.

hook: A 1930s colony of summer cottages and flower plots near the city centre, with one cottage preserved as a museum.
facts: The Vallila Allotment Garden was founded in 1932 and is the third oldest in Helsinki and the closest to the city centre.
It covers about 7.4 hectares with 169 plots; cottages are capped at 26 square metres.
Allotment gardens of the 1930s were intended to give lower-income city dwellers a place to grow vegetables and to improve health amid food shortages.
A period cottage operates as a small museum recreating 1930s allotment life, open Sundays in summer.
The public park area is open to all from May to September; individual plots remain private.
w: A 1930s colony of tiny summer cottages and flower plots, the closest allotment garden to the city centre. Founded in 1932 to give poorer families a patch to grow food, its 169 plots cap cottages at 26 square metres. One is kept as a museum of 1930s allotment life, open summer Sundays. A pocket countryside, ten minutes from downtown.

hook: A speakeasy hidden behind an unmarked door on Katariinankatu, entered only by ringing a bell.
facts: The bar is at Katariinankatu 3 in the Kruununhaka district of central Helsinki.
Entry is through an unmarked door; guests ring a bell and wait for a host to seat them.
Its theme draws on Finland's prohibition era of 1919-1932, when alcohol moved through hidden venues known by code names.
It is open Monday-Thursday 17:00-01:00 and Friday-Saturday 16:00-02:00, confirmed operating in 2026.
w: A speakeasy behind an unmarked door on Katariinankatu -- you ring a bell and wait to be let in and seated. The theme is Finland's own dry years, 1919 to 1932, when drink moved through hidden rooms known by code names. No sign, no menu in the window, just the bell. A prohibition fantasy, played straight.

hook: A cosy Kallio rum and tiki bar pouring tropical and classic cocktails.
facts: HogoHuone is located at Viides linja 7 in the Kallio district of Helsinki.
It specialises in rum, with original, classic and tropical (tiki-style) cocktails.
Opening hours are Monday-Thursday 18:00-02:00 and Friday-Saturday 17:00-02:00.
It was featured in Falstaff's 2026 bar guide for Finland, confirming it is open in 2026.
w: A cosy rum and tiki bar in Kallio, pouring tropical drinks and classic cocktails a long way from any beach. Tucked on Viides linja, it leans into the unlikely: palm-print warmth against the northern dark. Open late most nights. A slice of the South Pacific, smuggled into a Helsinki winter.

hook: A tiny corner bar inside a 1910s wooden cottage in the Puu-Vallila wooden-house quarter.
facts: Pikku-Vallila is at Vallilantie 19 in Helsinki's Vallila district.
The bar was founded in 1986 and occupies a wooden cottage built in the 1910s in the Puu-Vallila wooden-house area.
It opens daily, from 15:00 (16:00 at weekends), with late hours on Friday and Saturday.
Listings updated into 2026 confirm it is still operating.
w: A tiny corner bar inside a 1910s wooden cottage, in the Puu-Vallila quarter of old timber houses. Going since 1986, it seats barely a handful among the painted clapboard. A neighbourhood local in a building that outlived the city's wooden age. Small, warm and easy to walk straight past.

hook: Helsinki's old red-brick municipal abattoir, now a food, distillery and event yard
facts: The slaughterhouse buildings were designed by architect Bertel Liljequist and opened in 1933.
The municipal slaughterhouse closed in 1992; the brick complex then housed smaller butchers, bakeries and food businesses.
The area was officially opened to the general public as an urban-culture space in autumn 2012.
It is managed by Tukkutori (the City of Helsinki's wholesale market) and home to more than a dozen operators including restaurants, a distillery and coffee roasters.
Its address is Työpajankatu 2, in the Hermanni district next to Kalasatama.
w: Helsinki's old municipal abattoir -- a 1933 red-brick complex by Bertel Liljequist -- now a food and distillery yard humming with weekend life. The slaughterhouse closed in 1992; butchers and bakers crept into the empty halls, and in 2012 the city threw the courtyard open. A dozen-plus roasters, restaurants and a distillery fill it now.

hook: A former power plant and gasworks turned culture hub with Finland's first legal graffiti wall
facts: A steam-turbine power plant was completed here in 1909 and a gas plant in 1910; the city produced gas and electricity at Suvilahti into the 2000s.
The main buildings were designed in Viennese Art Nouveau style by architect Selim A. Lindqvist (1867–1939).
Two large steel-and-brick gasholders survive as city landmarks, administered by the City of Helsinki.
Finland's first authorised graffiti wall, roughly 100 metres long, opened here in 2009 for Suvilahti's energy-production centenary.
The complex now hosts cultural tenants such as Cirko – Center for New Circus and the Tiivistämö event venue.
Its address is Kaasutehtaankatu 1 / Sörnäisten rantatie 22, in the Sörnäinen district.
w: A 1909 power plant and gasworks, all Viennese Art Nouveau brick by Selim Lindqvist, turned culture hub -- and home to Finland's first legal graffiti wall, a hundred metres of it opened in 2009. Two great steel gasholders still loom as landmarks. New-circus and event venues fill the old industry; skaters and writers claim the rest.

hook: An architect-designed, wood-fired public sauna on the sea at Hakaniemenranta
facts: It was created by architect Tuomas Toivonen and artist Nene Tsuboi and opened to the public in 2013.
The compact one-storey white building has candle-lit rooms and a wood-pellet–fired stove.
Bathers can cool off directly in the sea from the shore at Hakaniemenranta.
Its address is Hakaniemenranta 17, on the edge of the Merihaka district facing Hakaniemi.
It is described as the first new public sauna built in central Helsinki in decades, part of a 2010s seaside-sauna revival.
w: An architect-designed public sauna on the sea at Hakaniemenranta -- the first new public sauna in central Helsinki in decades, opened 2013. Tuomas Toivonen and Nene Tsuboi gave it candle-lit rooms and a wood-pellet stove; bathers cool off straight into the sea off the shore. The clean, quiet end of the city's sauna revival.

hook: Helsinki's last traditional wood-heated public sauna, steaming since 1928
facts: The sauna building was completed in 1928 and is the oldest surviving public sauna in Helsinki.
It is the only remaining traditional wood-heated public sauna in the city.
It has separate wood-heated public sections for men (ground floor) and women (first floor), plus a bookable private sauna upstairs.
It was renovated in 1999 in a tradition-respecting manner and has been family-owned by the Holopainen family since 1991.
Its address is Harjutorinkatu 1, in the Kallio district.
w: Helsinki's last traditional wood-heated public sauna, steaming since 1928 -- the oldest left in the city. Separate men's and women's sections fire up with real wood, the way the whole city once bathed; regulars cool off on the street wrapped in towels. The Holopainen family have run it since 1991. Old-school Kallio ritual, smoke and all.

hook: Finland's oldest indoor swimming hall, a 1928 Nordic Classical / Art Deco bathing temple
facts: It was inaugurated on 4 June 1928 as the first and oldest public indoor swimming hall in Finland.
It was designed by architect Väinö Vähäkallio, who was inspired by Stockholm's Centralbadet and Central European spas.
Its style is 1920s Nordic Classicism enriched with Art Deco influences.
The original six-floor hall included two pools, a diving platform, a restaurant, a dance school, a tennis hall and a physical-therapy centre.
It reopened to the public on 2 February 2026 after a comprehensive renovation.
It sits on Yrjönkatu in the Kamppi district of central Helsinki.
w: Finland's oldest indoor swimming hall, a 1928 bathing temple modelled on Stockholm's Centralbadet. The grand original packed in two pools, a diving platform, a dance school and a tennis hall over six floors. Separate days for men and women, swimsuits long optional. Reopened in 2026 after a deep restoration.

hook: A former military fortress island between Suomenlinna and Santahamina, reclaimed by nature
facts: The Finnish Defence Forces announced their departure from the island in 2008.
From 2013 Metsähallitus (National Parks Finland) prepared the island for visitors, and it opened to the public in May 2016.
Over 400 plant species grow on the island, and its old craters, bunkers and caverns shelter rare mosses, lichens, bats and birds.
Marked walking routes include Alexander's tour (about 3 km) and the adjoining Kuninkaansaari tour (about 2.5 km).
It is reached by ferry from Helsinki's Market Square, roughly a 20-minute crossing, in the May–September season.
It lies in the archipelago between Suomenlinna and Santahamina.
w: A former military fortress island between Suomenlinna and Santahamina, off-limits for a century, now reclaimed by the wild. The army left in 2008; the island opened to the public in 2016. Over 400 plant species grow over its craters and bunkers; bats roost in the caverns. Marked trails loop it. A closed-off arsenal, gone gloriously feral.

hook: A tiny former mine-clearance island, now a summer island with a public wood-fired sauna
facts: Lonna was used by the Finnish Defence Forces until the early 21st century and opened to the public in 2014.
In the 1800s it held gunpowder and mine storage and production, later serving as a mine clean-up centre and a ship-demagnetising station.
It hosts a summer restaurant, an event space and a public sauna.
The public sauna, designed by OOPEAA, opened in 2017 and has two loft saunas heated by wood-burning stoves.
The island sits opposite the Suomenlinna passenger terminal, just off central Helsinki.
legend: Lonna is said to have been the site of negotiations between Swedish and Russian powers in 1808 during the Finnish War.
w: A tiny island off the Market Square that once stored gunpowder and swept mines for the navy, opened to the public only in 2014. A summer restaurant and event hall fill it now, and an OOPEAA public sauna of 2017 heats two loft rooms by wood. Tradition even puts a Swedish-Russian parley here in 1808. The smallest hop into the archipelago.
